attributes.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents to the labour ward at 36 weeks gestation with regular contractions. She has a dichorionic diamniotic twin pregnancy. She has no significant medical history and takes only pregnancy supplements. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her abdomen is soft with a longitudinal lie of both twins. Vaginal examination reveals she is 4 cm dilated. You are asked to commence cardiotocography monitoring.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate approach to cardiotocography monitoring in this situation?
options: Set both fetal heart rate recordings to the same range
Use a single transducer to monitor both twins simultaneously
Monitor Twin 1 for 30 minutes then switch to Twin 2
Offset the fetal heart rates by 20 beats/min with clear labelling
attributes.explanation:
In twin pregnancies during labour, it is essential to monitor both fetuses continuously and be able to differentiate between their individual heart rate patterns. This is because twin pregnancies are high risk and would not be classified as an "uncomplicated pregnancy" where continuous monitoring is not routinely recommended. This allows for separate assessment of each twin's wellbeing and early detection of compromise in either fetus.

Offsetting the fetal heart rates by 20 beats/min with clear labelling is the most appropriate approach. This involves setting one twin's trace to display 20 beats/min higher than the other, with clear documentation of which trace belongs to which twin. This separation allows for easy visual distinction between the twins while maintaining continuous monitoring of both.

Monitoring Twin 1 for 30 minutes then switching to Twin 2 is inappropriate as it leaves one twin unmonitored for extended periods. During labour, continuous monitoring of both twins is essential as fetal compromise can develop rapidly.

Setting both fetal heart rate recordings to the same range makes it difficult to distinguish between the twins' traces and could lead to confusion in interpretation, especially if their baseline heart rates are similar.

Using a single transducer to monitor both twins simultaneously is technically inadequate as it cannot reliably distinguish between the two fetal heart rates and may miss periods of compromise in one twin.

Alternating between twins every 15 minutes is unsafe as it leaves one twin unmonitored for significant periods during labour.

When interpreting the CTG after setting an offset, it's crucial to remember to subtract 20 beats/min from the apparent baseline of the offset trace to determine the true fetal heart rate. For example, if the offset trace shows a baseline of 160, the true baseline is 140 beats/min.
learningPoints: Twin cardiotocography (CTG) monitoring requires clear differentiation of fetal heart rates, typically by setting a 20 beats/min offset between the recordings.
Each twin's CTG trace must be clearly labelled to ensure correct interpretation of individual fetal wellbeing.
When interpreting offset CTG traces, the artificial adjustment must be considered when determining the true baseline heart rate.
references: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE). Twin and triplet pregnancy [Internet]. London: NICE; 2019 [cited 2024 Dec 4]. Available from: https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ng137/resources/twin-and-triplet-pregnancy-pdf-66141724389829.

Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RANZCOG). Intrapartum fetal surveillance: Clinical guideline [Internet]. East Melbourne, VIC: RANZCOG; 2021 [cited 2024 Dec 4]. Available from: https://ranzcog.edu.au/wp-content/uploads/Intrapartum-Fetal-Surveillance.pdf.
